questions.q: Copyright protection primarily grants authors the exclusive right to:
options: Patent their inventions
Reproduce, distribute, and publicly display their original works
Trademark their business names
Maintain trade secrets indefinitely
questions.explanation: Copyright gives creators exclusive rights to reproduce, distribute, and display their original creative works. Patents protect inventions, while trademarks protect brand names.

questions.q: Which type of intellectual property protects new inventions or technological innovations?
options: Copyright
Trademark
Patent
Trade secret
questions.explanation: Patents protect inventions and technological innovations for a limited period (typically 20 years). They require disclosure of the invention in exchange for exclusive rights to make, use, or sell it.

questions.q: What is the primary purpose of a trademark in intellectual property law?
options: To protect creative expression like novels and music
To protect the design of manufactured goods
To identify and distinguish goods or services from competitors
To keep business formulas and processes secret
questions.explanation: Trademarks identify and distinguish a company's goods or services from others, protecting brand identity. Copyright protects creative works, while patents protect inventions.

questions.q: A trade secret is best defined as:
options: Any information about a business
Information that provides economic advantage, is kept secret, and has reasonable measures to protect it
Any patented technology
Information disclosed to the public domain
questions.explanation: Trade secrets are valuable information protected by keeping them confidential (like Coca-Cola's formula). Unlike patents, they don't expire if the secrecy is maintained, but disclosure can mean loss of protection.

questions.q: The 'fair use' doctrine in copyright law permits:
options: Using copyrighted material without any limitation
Limited use of copyrighted material for purposes like criticism, education, or parody without permission
Complete copying of any published work
Using copyrighted material commercially
questions.explanation: Fair use allows limited use of copyrighted material for criticism, commentary, education, and parody without permission or payment. Factors include the purpose, nature of work, amount used, and market effect.

questions.q: How long does copyright protection typically last in most modern legal systems?
options: 10 years from publication
The lifetime of the author plus 50 to 70 years
Indefinitely, as long as the copyright owner renews it
25 years from first publication
questions.explanation: Copyright typically extends for the author's life plus 50-70 years (varies by jurisdiction). This provides long-term protection for creators and their heirs, but eventually works enter the public domain.

questions.q: What must a patent applicant disclose to the Patent Office?
options: Only the patent claims without details
Full details of the invention enabling someone skilled to reproduce it
The commercial price of the invention
Only the inventor's name and address
questions.explanation: Patent applicants must provide enabling disclosure - enough detail that someone skilled in the field could reproduce the invention. This exchange of disclosure for exclusive rights is foundational to patent law.

questions.q: Dilution in trademark law refers to:
options: Weakening a trademark's strength through genericide or blurring
Reducing the price of trademarked goods
Voluntary licensing of a trademark
Registering similar trademarks
questions.explanation: Trademark dilution occurs when a mark's distinctiveness is weakened through genericide (becoming generic) or blurring (association with unrelated goods). This harms the mark's ability to identify source.

questions.q: Which IP protection requires the least formality to secure rights?
options: Patents (require application and examination)
Trademarks (require registration)
Copyright (automatic upon creation without registration)
Trade secrets (require government filing)
questions.explanation: Copyright protection is automatic upon creation of original works without requiring registration or formal notice. Patents, trademarks, and trade secrets typically require more formal steps to secure protection.

questions.q: The concept of 'moral rights' in intellectual property primarily protects:
options: The right to make money from a work
An author's reputation and integrity in their creative work
The manufacturer's right to produce goods
Government censorship rights
questions.explanation: Moral rights protect creators' non-economic interests: the right of attribution (being credited) and the right of integrity (preventing distortion of the work). These exist separately from economic copyright rights in many jurisdictions.
